Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe flip through some readings when I felt like it, but nothing consistent. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep or when I suddenly remembered something was due. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't get myself to care enough to build a real schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks barely looking at anything, then cram when a deadline was breathing down my neck. Sometimes I'd have work shifts or stuff going on with friends that took priority, and honestly, the course just fell to the bottom of the list. Regular study times weren't really my thing this semester.
Q3: Usually a deadline notification or someone mentioning an assignment in our group chat would get me to log in. I wasn't one of those people who checked the LMS daily or anything close to that. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I'd missed something major, but that was rare. It was almost always reactive, never proactive.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd use it when I absolutely had to, but it never felt like a natural part of my day. I know some people had it bookmarked and checked it constantly, but I just couldn't get into that rhythm. It always felt like a chore I was avoiding.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually kind of useful because I could see what other people were thinking about the material. When I did watch the videos, they helped more than the readings because I could just listen and absorb. But honestly, I engaged with resources so sporadically that it's hard to say anything felt consistently useful to me.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the optional stuff entirely. Extra readings, supplementary videos, anything that wasn't directly tied to an assignment grade—I skipped it. With limited time and energy, I had to pick my battles, and those just didn't make the cut. I figured I'd circle back to them but never did.
Q7: If it was going to directly impact my grade or if it was something I could finish quickly, I'd do it. Anything that felt like busywork or took too long for unclear benefit, I'd skip. I needed to see a clear connection between the effort and the outcome, otherwise my brain just wouldn't engage with it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely ignorable. Required activities had this weight to them because of grades, but optional ones? My brain just categorized them as 'nice to have' and then promptly forgot about them. There's already so much required stuff that adding optional work on top felt overwhelming.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone and look at them during breaks at work or on the bus. I'd also ask friends to explain concepts to me instead of rewatching lectures. A lot of my learning happened through conversations and borrowed notes rather than anything the LMS would track.
Q10: There were times I'd sit with a classmate for an hour going over material, and none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd read through printed notes while waiting for something. The LMS only sees what happens on the platform, but a lot of my studying—what little there was—happened completely offline.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a reading or save a video link, then never go back to the LMS for it. I'd study from those downloaded files on my phone during commutes or slow periods at work. The LMS would show I accessed it once, but I might have reviewed that material multiple times offline.
Q12: Midway through the semester, things got really hard. I was stressed about money, picking up extra shifts at work, and just feeling disconnected from the whole university experience. Going to class felt pointless when I was exhausted, and the course material started feeling abstract and irrelevant to my actual life problems.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was working more hours or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS was the first thing to get dropped. I'd feel too mentally drained to engage with course content after a long shift. Competing responsibilities definitely won out over logging into the platform most of the time.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while scrolling on my phone or have a reading open while my mind was completely elsewhere. I was technically 'engaging' with the material, but nothing was actually sinking in. It was more about going through the motions than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams constantly, even though I knew they mattered most for my grade. There's something about exam prep that just felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid it until the last possible moment. Assignments were easier to tackle because they had concrete steps, but open-ended studying? I'd put that off indefinitely.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline coming up and suddenly become productive for a day or two. Without that pressure, I'd drift and not engage at all. My entire LMS usage pattern was probably just a series of deadline-driven spikes rather than steady engagement.
Q17: When I got feedback, it was usually too late for me to really do anything with it. By the time I saw comments on one assignment, I was already scrambling for the next deadline. I appreciated the peer support stuff, but feedback didn't really change my behavior much because I was always in reactive mode.
Q18: The structure felt pretty standard, I guess. Weekly modules with deadlines spread throughout. But I wasn't attending classes regularly, so I'd often miss context that made the structure make sense. Without that foundation, the LMS content sometimes felt disconnected and harder to follow on my own.
Q19: The fact that I could access materials anytime was helpful given my weird schedule. But the course assumed you were keeping up week by week, and once I fell behind, catching up felt impossible. There wasn't really a good way to jump back in if you'd missed several weeks of content.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and keeps checking things without actually completing work, or someone who's confused and keeps revisiting materials trying to understand. It could also be someone like me who opens stuff with good intentions but then gets distracted or overwhelmed and doesn't follow through.
Q21: That kind of describes me in a way—low LMS activity but I still managed to complete most assignments. I did a lot of work offline or got help from peers. The LMS didn't capture all the ways I was engaging with the material, even if my exam score shows I wasn't learning as deeply as I needed to.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if your mind was elsewhere. They miss offline studying, peer discussions, and the quality of engagement. They're good for tracking activity but terrible for measuring actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just complete it. It's when the material feels relevant and you're mentally present while studying. I know I wasn't really engaged this semester—I was just surviving and checking boxes when I had to.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with financial stress, working long hours, and feeling pretty disconnected from the whole university thing. It wouldn't show the conversations I had with classmates trying to catch up, or the times I studied from downloaded materials. It just shows clicks, not context.
